mūla.devanāgarī: एतां दृष्टिम् अवष्टभ्य नष्टात्मानो ऽल्प-बुद्धयः | प्रभवन्त्य् उग्र-कर्माणः क्षयाय जगतो ऽहिताः
mūla.iast: etāṃ dṛṣṭim avaṣṭabhya naṣṭātmāno 'lpa-buddhayaḥ | prabhavanty ugra-karmāṇaḥ kṣayāya jagato 'hitāḥ
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 16 (Daivāsura-Sampad-Vibhāga-Yoga (The Yoga of Distinction Between Divine and Demonic Endowments)), verse 9
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: अवष्टभ्य आश्रित्य नष्टात्मानः नष्टस्वभावाः विभ्रष्टपरलोकसाधनाः अल्पबुद्धयः विषयविषया अल्पैव बुद्धिः येषां ते अल्पबुद्धय
अवष्टभ्य अवलम्ब्य? नष्टात्मानः? अदृष्टदेहातिरिक्तात्मानः? अल्पबुद्धयः -- घटादिवद् ज्ञेयभूते देहे ज्ञातृत्वेन देहव्यतिरि
आश्रित्य नष्टात्मानः नष्टस्वभावाः विभ्रष्टपरलोकसाधनाः अल्पबुद्धयः विषयविषया अल्पैव बुद्धिः येषां ते अल्पबुद्धयः प्रभवन्
अवलम्ब्य? नष्टात्मानः? अदृष्टदेहातिरिक्तात्मानः? अल्पबुद्धयः -- घटादिवद् ज्ञेयभूते देहे ज्ञातृत्वेन देहव्यतिरिक्त आत्मा

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: Those who cling to this materialist view — that there is no ātman (self) beyond the body, no dharma (right order), no loka (world beyond) — have lost their svabhāva (essential nature) and degraded the very instrument by which liberation is won. With intellect limited to sense-objects alone, they arise as beings of fierce and violent action, hostile to the world. Śaṅkara reads 'naṣṭātmānaḥ' (those whose self is lost) as vinaṣṭa-paraloka-sādhana — they have destroyed the very means to transcendence — and 'prabhavanty' as their literal arising in the world for its ruin.
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkara: naṣṭasvabhāvāḥ vibhraṣṭaparalokasādhanāḥ — 'those whose nature is destroyed, whose means to the other world are ruined'; alpabuddhayaḥ viṣayaviṣayā — intellect bounded entirely by objects; jagataḥ śatravaḥ — enemies of the world.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: Those who have never discerned the ātman (self) distinct from the deha (body) — who cannot see, as Rāmānuja observes, that a knower cannot be identical to what it knows — are naṣṭātmānaḥ (those in whom the self is lost to sight). Unable to perceive Bhagavān as the inner ground of all existence, they act only through himsa (violence) and become instruments of the world's undoing. The failure here is vivekākuśalatā — incompetence in discernment — which blocks entry into kainkarya (loving service) altogether.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja: adṛṣṭadeha-atirikta-ātmānaḥ — 'those who have not seen the self beyond the body'; ghaṭādivad jñeyabhūte dehe jñātṛtvena deha-vyatirikta ātmā na upalabhyate — the self as knower is not found in the body as object; vivekākuśalāḥ — unskilled in discrimination.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: In Dvaita the eternal distinction between jīva (individual self), jagat (world), and Hari (the Lord) is the very structure reality refuses to collapse. Those who deny this threefold distinction — holding all to be one undifferentiated matter — become naṣṭātmānaḥ (those whose self is annihilated in error) and, acting from ugra-karma (fierce deeds) rooted in that denial, bring destruction upon a world that is Hari's own body of sovereignty. No specific verse-commentary by Madhva survives for 16.9; this rendering extrapolates from Madhva's consistent polemical framing of the nāstika (denier) as the archetype of adharma (disorder).
dvaita.divergence_note: NOTE: Madhva's commentary on BG 16.9 is absent from the panel ('Sri Madhvacharya did not comment on this sloka'). Rendering extrapolated from Madhva's documented position on nāstika philosophy and jīva-Brahman nitya-bheda (eternal difference).

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Vallabha reads the verse through the lens of Puṣṭi-mārga grace: those who adopt the mithyātva-dṛṣṭi (view of falseness-of-all) — even if in the distorted guise of Advaita — and who believe the jīva (individual soul) is bound to Brahman only by avidyā (ignorance), have in fact lost their true identity as Kṛṣṇa's own. They become naṣṭātmānaḥ (self-lost), precisely because they reject the eternal svarūpa (intrinsic form) that Kṛṣṇa's prasāda (grace) would have revealed. Vallabha's citation of Viṣṇupurāṇa grounds the verse's 'kṣaya' (decay) within cosmic cycles of āvirbhāva-tirobhāva (manifestation-withdrawal), not nihilistic ending.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha: sarvatra mṛṣātva-dṛṣṭiṃ cāvaṣṭabhya — 'clinging to a view of universal falseness'; avidyāsambandha-jīvatva-mata-aṅgīkārāt naṣṭātmānaḥ — self-lost through accepting the doctrine that the jīva is related to Brahman only via avidyā; tathābhūtasya jagataḥ kṣayāya udbhavanti — they arise for the decay of such a world.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Śrīdhara Svāmī reads the verse as a sociological portrait of the Lokāyatika (materialist) in action: clinging to the Cārvāka dṛṣṭi (materialist view), their citta (mind-field) is malinacitta (defiled), their intellect contracted to dṛṣṭārtha-mātra (only visible ends), and they become ahita (enemies) who proliferate ugra-karma (fierce deeds) across the world. The bhakti reading locates the root damage in the loss of śraddhā (faith) in the unseen — without which any orientation toward Bhagavān becomes impossible.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara: lokāyatikānāṃ dṛṣṭim āśritya naṣṭātmāno malinacittāḥ — 'self-lost, with defiled minds, having taken the materialist view'; dṛṣṭārthamātramatayas — 'whose understanding extends only to the visible'; ahitā vairiṇo bhūtvā — 'becoming enemies and foes'.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana anticipates the objection that this Lokāyatika view might itself have scriptural standing — and refutes it sharply: the view is no śāstrīya-dṛṣṭi (scripture-grounded vision) but a trap that causes rebirth in lower forms (vyāghra-sarpa-ādi-rūpeṇa — as tigers, serpents, and the like). Those who hold it become naṣṭātmānaḥ (self-ruined), motivated only by dṛṣṭa-mātra-uddeśa (visible-world goals alone), and arise for the jagat's kṣaya (dissolution). For the mumukṣu (seeker of liberation) and the bhakta (devotee) alike, this view is heyā (to be entirely abandoned) as the supreme obstacle to śreyas (the highest good).
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: iyaṃ dṛṣṭiḥ śāstrīya-dṛṣṭivad iṣṭaiva iti āśaṅkya — 'anticipating the doubt that this view might be welcome like a scriptural view'; vyāghasarpādirūpeṇa prabhavanty — 'they arise in the form of tigers, serpents, etc.'; atyanta-adhogati-hetutayā sarvātmanā śreyorthhibhir heyā — 'to be wholly abandoned by all who seek the highest good'.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If the naṣṭātmā (self-lost person) has lost access to their own essential nature, what remains as the site of their action — and who or what bears moral responsibility for ugra-karma (fierce deeds) done from that site?

All six schools agree that a false ontology produces destructive action — but they disagree on what is lost: is it the transcendent ātman, the relational jīva, or the svarūpa (intrinsic form) given by grace? What is actually at stake in that disagreement?

Śaṅkara says alpabuddhayaḥ (little-intellect) means intellect limited to viṣaya (sense-objects) — is this a cognitive diagnosis, a moral diagnosis, or both? Can someone reason brilliantly within the materialist frame and still be alpabuddhayaḥ?

The verse says such persons 'arise' (prabhavanty) for the world's kṣaya (decay) — is this karmic determinism, a sociological observation, or a warning about cultural contagion? How does each school's answer change the prescription?

Madhusūdana says this view produces rebirth as tigers and serpents — is that metaphorical (the violent disposition becomes one's new nature) or literal (punarbhava in lower janmas)? What does each reading demand of us practically?

The bhāṣyas consistently identify himsa (violence) as the behavioral signature of this view — but contemporary ugra-karma (fierce action) is often institutional, algorithmic, or structural rather than bodily. Does the verse's diagnosis still apply, and how?

If Vallabha is right that even a distorted Advaita (mithyātva-dṛṣṭi misapplied) can produce naṣṭātmatā (self-lostness), what distinguishes liberating non-attachment from nihilistic world-denial in everyday practice?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you catch yourself making a purely cost-benefit calculation — treating relationships, creative work, or ethical commitments as inputs and outputs with no intrinsic reality — notice: you are temporarily operating from alpa-buddhi (contracted intellect). The Advaita corrective is not to add sentiment, but to ask what the witness behind the calculation is. Even a brief pause to locate the śākṣin (witness) breaks the naṣṭātmatā (self-lostness) before it generates ugra-karma (fierce action) in the world.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Rāmānuja's vivekākuśalatā (incompetence in discernment) is diagnosable in professional life: you mistake the role you perform for the person you are. The Viśiṣṭādvaita application is to cultivate kainkarya-buddhi (servant-of-Bhagavān intelligence) in the role itself — not by adding ritual, but by asking 'whose work is this really serving?' before each significant decision. The role becomes a window, not a wall.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Dvaita's eternal distinction — jīva (individual) is never Hari (the Lord), and conflating them is the root error — translates into a daily discipline against spiritual bypassing: do not use 'we are all one' as cover for avoiding accountability. Each jīva is responsible precisely because it is distinct. The practical application is radical ownership: your harm is yours, your action is yours, and no metaphysical merger dissolves that.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's insight is that grace (prasāda) is the primary protection against naṣṭātmatā (self-lostness) — not effort alone. The everyday application in Puṣṭi-mārga terms: begin each day with the acknowledgment that Kṛṣṇa's svarūpa (intrinsic form) is already present in your own nature, not waiting to be earned. Actions taken from that ground — even mundane ones — become expressions of līlā (divine play) rather than instruments of kṣaya (decay).
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's malinacitta (defiled mind) is not a moral judgment but a technical description: a citta clogged by dṛṣṭārtha-mātra (only visible ends) cannot register the presence of Bhagavān in what it encounters. The bhakti application is a daily citta-śodhana (mind-purification) practice — not elaborate ritual, but the deliberate cultivation of śraddhā (faith in the unseen) as a counterweight to the transactional reflex. Even one genuine moment of 'this matters beyond its use to me' is that śodhana.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's sharpest warning is that this view (lokāyatika-dṛṣṭi) disguises itself as wisdom — it can sound like scientific rigor, pragmatic clarity, or even non-attachment. The advaita-bhakti application is discernment of the difference between vairāgya (dispassion born of seeing the Whole) and the materialist's flat rejection of the unseen. Ask: does this 'letting go' leave more space for Kṛṣṇa, or does it simply contract the world to what is measurable? That question is Madhusūdana's daily compass.
